items.mistake: Including terms where p(x) = 0 as 0 · log 0 without understanding the convention
items.fix: Sum only over x with p(x) > 0; treat the limit lim_{p→0+} p log p = 0 so zero-probability outcomes contribute nothing.
items.why: log 0 is undefined; the convention keeps H(X) finite and matches the definition as an expectation over outcomes that actually occur.
items.example: For p = [1.0, 0.0, 0.0], compute H = −1.0 · log₂(1.0) = 0, not an error from log(0).

items.mistake: Mixing log bases when comparing entropy values
items.fix: Pick one base (usually 2 for bits) and convert: H_nats = H_bits · ln 2. State the unit in every numeric answer.
items.why: A fair coin is 1 bit but ≈ 0.693 nats; comparing 1 to 0.693 without conversion leads to wrong conclusions.
items.example: Textbook gives H = ln 2 for a fair coin (nats); in bits that is 1.0, not ln 2 ≈ 0.693 bits.

items.mistake: Confusing Shannon entropy H(X) with thermodynamic entropy from physics
items.fix: Use H(X) = −Σ p log p for information uncertainty; physical entropy has units of energy/temperature and a different formal role despite the shared name.
items.why: Shannon borrowed the name for analogy, but formulas, units, and applications differ; conflating them causes unit and interpretation errors.
items.example: A fair coin has H = 1 bit, not joules per kelvin—keep information entropy dimensionless in chosen bit/nat units.

items.mistake: Using raw counts instead of probabilities in the entropy formula
items.fix: Normalize counts to a PMF that sums to 1 before applying −Σ p log p, or use a library (e.g. sklearn.metrics.entropy) that accepts counts and normalizes internally.
items.why: Plugging counts c_i directly into −c log c does not yield a valid entropy and scales incorrectly with sample size.
items.example: Counts [50, 50] → p = [0.5, 0.5] → H = 1 bit; using 50 and 50 directly would give a meaningless large number.
